Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed simile-like prepositional/noun phrase headed by macam; idiom normally functions as predicate or comparison after jadi/serupa/macam; no tense marking.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Used for someone whose worldview is narrow because they lack exposure, travel, or experience beyond their immediate surroundings.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia kurang pengalaman dan belum banyak melihat dunia.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech; family advice; education; youth guidance; humorous teasing.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Shared Malay-world proverb adapted into Chetti Malay speech; the coconut-shell image resonates with Melaka household life and older oral teaching styles.
First Example Sentence: Lu jangan jadi macam katak bawah tempurung; pi dengar cerita orang lain juga.
Second Example Sentence: Budak itu pandai, tapi kalau dia tak keluar dari kampung, dia macam katak bawah tempurung.
Third Example Sentence: Mami bilang gua macam katak bawah tempurung sebab gua takut kerja di bandar.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause with metaphorical subject air and negated result tak putus; functions as a sentential proverb about kinship.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Family bonds remain even after quarrels, anger, or temporary separation.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Hubungan keluarga tetap kuat walaupun ada pertengkaran.
Usage Contexts: Family disputes; reconciliation; festival gatherings; elder counsel; community mediation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay kinship proverb used in Peranakan settings; suitable for Chetti contexts where extended family and ancestor gatherings reinforce belonging.
First Example Sentence: Adik sama abang gaduh kuat, tapi air cincang tak putus, nyari mereka makan sama-sama.
Second Example Sentence: Lu jangan simpan marah lama pada sepupu; air cincang tak putus.
Third Example Sentence: Waktu Bhogi Parchu, mami ingatkan semua orang bahawa air cincang tak putus.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed proverb-like clause with concessive particle pun; animate subject ulat plus locative phrase dalam batu marks survival under constraint.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Even in hardship, a person can survive by adapting and working patiently.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Orang yang susah masih boleh mencari jalan untuk hidup.
Usage Contexts: Resilience; small business; hardship; migration; moral advice; elder storytelling.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Shared Malay proverb particularly apt for minority-language and trading-community histories, where adaptation and endurance are valued.
First Example Sentence: Kedai kecil itu susah masa hujan, tapi ulat dalam batu pun hidup.
Second Example Sentence: Gua tak ada banyak duit, namun gua usaha; ulat dalam batu pun hidup.
Third Example Sentence: Mami selalu bilang, ulat dalam batu pun hidup kalau kita tak malas.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjectival idiom formed from adjective panjang plus body-part noun tangan; functions as a predicate or post-nominal descriptor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Describes someone who steals, takes things without permission, or cannot be trusted with property.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia suka mengambil barang orang tanpa izin.
Usage Contexts: Warnings; marketplace talk; household discipline; gossip; security notices.
Register and Tone: Colloquial, accusatory, cautionary; can be insulting.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common Malay bodily metaphor retained in Malay-based creole speech; useful for everyday warnings around shops, homes, and communal events.
First Example Sentence: Jaga mangkok tembaga itu, orang panjang tangan sudah masuk pasar.
Second Example Sentence: Mereka tak panggil dia tolong jual kuih sebab dia panjang tangan.
Third Example Sentence: Bibik marah budak yang panjang tangan ambil duit tabung kuil.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase idiom with makan plus object garam and temporal adverb dulu; usually takes sudah to mark accumulated experience.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is older or more experienced and therefore has practical wisdom.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia lebih berpengalaman daripada orang muda.
Usage Contexts: Respect for elders; workplace mentoring; family advice; apprenticeship; market trade.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay metaphor of salt as life experience; aligns with Chetti elder authority in family, temple, food, and trade domains.
First Example Sentence: Dengar cakap thaltha; dia sudah makan garam dulu dalam kerja kebun.
Second Example Sentence: Gua baru mula berniaga, mami sudah makan garam dulu di pasar Melaka.
Third Example Sentence: Lu orang jangan ketawa pada nasihat mama, dia makan garam dulu.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Parallel body-part construction: noun phrase mulut manis contrasted with hati lain; compressed clause without overt copula.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks kindly or flatteringly but hides a different intention or dishonest feeling.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia bercakap baik tetapi tidak ikhlas.
Usage Contexts: Sarcasm; social caution; workplace politics; family gossip; negotiation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay moral expression shaped by oral advice culture; in creole use, hati may surface phonetically without /h/ while retaining standard spelling.
First Example Sentence: Dia mulut manis hati lain; depan mami puji, belakang dia bilang buruk.
Second Example Sentence: Lu jangan cepat percaya pelanggan itu, mulut manis hati lain.
Third Example Sentence: Dalam mesyuarat, dia mulut manis hati lain sampai semua orang keliru.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Noun phrase metaphor with head ayam and modifier tambatan; functions as a predicate nominal for a trusted main performer or relied-upon person.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The dependable key person whom everyone relies on for success.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia orang utama yang paling dipercayai untuk tugas itu.
Usage Contexts: Community events; temple work; sports; business teams; family responsibilities.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rural Malay metaphor absorbed into local speech; relevant to kampung organization, temple service, and community event leadership.
First Example Sentence: Untuk urus maalai kuil, bibik itu ayam tambatan kita.
Second Example Sentence: Pasukan kampung menang sebab anak mami jadi ayam tambatan.
Third Example Sentence: Bila ada majlis ubayam, semua orang cari mama kerana dia ayam tambatan.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed simile with bagai plus coordinated noun phrase aur dengan tebing; used as a predicate of mutual dependence.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Two people or groups support each other so closely that each depends on the other.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Mereka saling membantu dan memerlukan satu sama lain.
Usage Contexts: Neighbour relations; intercommunity cooperation; workplace teamwork; cultural preservation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay ecological proverb; especially meaningful in Melaka Peranakan contexts where Chetti, Baba, Malay, and other communities share neighbourhood histories.
First Example Sentence: Keluarga Chetti sama jiran Baba hidup bagai aur dengan tebing di lorong itu.
Second Example Sentence: Gua masak kari, lu siapkan nasi; kita bagai aur dengan tebing.
Third Example Sentence: Dalam kerja bersih kuil, lu orang kena bagai aur dengan tebing.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed proverb-like noun phrase plus directional verb pulang; idiom functions as a predicate for return, fit, or restoration to origin.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person or thing returns to its rightful place, roots, family, or original community.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia kembali ke tempat asal atau keadaan yang sesuai.
Usage Contexts: Heritage return; diaspora; language revitalization; literature; ceremonial speech.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Malay sirih/betel imagery links to Peranakan ceremonial culture; in Chetti contexts it can frame return to Kampung Chetti, ancestral practice, or heritage language.
First Example Sentence: Lepas lama di Singapura, dia balik Kampung Chetti macam sirih pulang ke gangang.
Second Example Sentence: Bila gua dengar pantun thaltha, hati gua rasa sirih pulang ke gangang.
Third Example Sentence: Anak muda itu belajar bahasa rumah, macam sirih pulang ke gangang.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjectival compound metaphor formed from body-part noun kepala plus material noun batu; functions as predicate adjective.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone is stubborn, hard to advise, or unwilling to change their mind.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Dia degil dan tidak mahu menerima nasihat.
Usage Contexts: Family scolding; friendship teasing; workplace frustration; everyday criticism.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common Malay body-and-object metaphor; natural in informal Chetti Malay speech where direct kinship advice is often softened by humour.
First Example Sentence: Lu kepala batu; mami sudah bilang jangan pi sana waktu hujan.
Second Example Sentence: Adik kepala batu, tetap mahu buka kedai walaupun badan penat.
Third Example Sentence: Kalau semua kepala batu, kerja ubayam tak jalan elok.
